content.body: Every prescription, whether handwritten or electronic, contains a set of standard elements. Understanding each element is essential for accurate dispensing and for identifying incomplete or potentially fraudulent prescriptions. While the format may vary slightly between islands, the core components are consistent across the Caribbean.

rows: Name, qualifications, registration number, address, contact
Dr. Sarah Maraj, MBBS, Reg. #4521, 45 Frederick Street, POS
Full name, date of birth or age, address

content.body: A prescription is a formal medical instruction from a licensed healthcare provider to a pharmacist. To be legally valid and accurately filled, every prescription must include seven standard parts: patient information, prescriber details, the date, medication name and strength, dispensing instructions, directions for the patient, and the prescriber's signature.
The seven key components include:
1. Patient Information
The full name, date of birth, and address of the patient. This ensures the medication is prepared for the correct individual and helps prevent errors.
2. Prescriber Information
The name, title, address, phone number, and professional credentials (such as an NPI or DEA number) of the doctor or healthcare provider
3. Date of the Prescription
The exact date the prescription was written. This establishes a timeline, ensuring the treatment plan is current and allows pharmacists to verify its legal validity.
4. Medication Details (Inscription)
The name of the medication (generic or brand) along with its specific strength or dosage.
5. Dispensing Instructions (Subscription)
Specific instructions directed to the pharmacist on how much of the medication to dispense in total (e.g., number of pills or volume of liquid).
6. Directions for the Patient (Signatura / Sig)
Instructions on how the patient should take the medication. This typically details the dosage, frequency, route of administration (e.g., by mouth), and the duration of the treatment.
7. Refills and Prescriber's Signature

The authorized signature of the prescriber (which validates the order), along with the number of allowed refills. For controlled substances, this section may also indicate that generic equivalents are prohibited
THE 5 R's of MEDICATION:
There are the five rights of medication:
The right patient, right medication, right dose, right route and right time.

Be alert for: prescriptions written in pencil (easily altered), missing prescriber registration numbers, patients requesting specific controlled substances by name and strength, prescriptions from prescribers you cannot verify, alterations or crossed-out information without the prescriber's counter-signature, and prescriptions presented significantly after the date written (especially for controlled substances).
content.title: Red Flags on Prescriptions
